unicode.value: Unit 5: Professional values and ethics - Session 1

Todas las imágenes incluidas en esta unidad han sido generadas mediante inteligencia artificial a través de la herramienta ChatGPT (OpenAI). Estas imágenes han sido creadas exclusivamente para uso educativo y no reproducen contenido preexistente. No están sujetas a derechos de autor de terceros y pueden ser utilizadas dentro del marco del proyecto educativo sin fines comerciales. Se aplican los términos de uso y licencias correspondientes al servicio de generación de imágenes de OpenAI, que permiten su uso para fines educativos y de desarrollo de materiales propios.
Unit 5: Professional values and ethics - Session 1

🎯 Session 1: Understanding Workplace Values

Welcome to Session 1

What makes a workplace truly ethical? In this session, you’ll discover the values that create respectful and fair environments. Through a reading on fostering ethical behavior, you’ll explore how companies go beyond rules to promote integrity with everyday actions. You’ll also learn vocabulary to describe core values like respect, integrity, and accountability, and identify problematic behaviors, such as gossip or bias.

Grammatically, you’ll review and practice modal verbs like must, should, ought to, and mustn’t to express workplace rules, expectations, and advice.

By the end of this session, you’ll be able to:

  • Discuss workplace values and behaviors with confidence.
  • Use modal verbs to give advice and outline rules.
  • Reflect on how ethics apply in real professional scenarios.

This session lays the groundwork for the unit, equipping you with the language and insights to navigate workplace dilemmas, offer recommendations, and communicate ethically in your future career.

🚀 Let’s get started!

📖 Unit 5 – Session 1: Introduction to Reading Activity

🎯 What Does Ethics Mean to You?

Imagine you’re starting your dream job. You’re skilled, motivated, and ready to make a difference. But what happens when you face a tough choice—like whether to speak up about a mistake or treat everyone fairly under pressure? Ethics isn’t just about rules; it’s about building a workplace where values like integrity and respect shine through every day.

In this session, you’ll read “Building an Ethical Company,” a text that reveals how workplaces can nurture ethical professionals. Before you dive in, let’s spark your thinking with a few questions to connect the topic to your own experiences and future career.

  • What does “being ethical” mean to you in a workplace? Think of one value (e.g., honesty, fairness) that matters most in your vocational field.
  • Have you ever seen or heard about an unethical situation at work or school, like gossip or favoritism? How did it affect the environment?
  • Why might young professionals need support to make ethical decisions? Consider a time you felt unsure about the “right” thing to do.

💡 Tip: Jot down your thoughts—these questions will help you connect with the reading’s ideas.

🚀 Ready to Explore?

Get ready to explore how companies can create ethical cultures through daily practices, mentoring, and open discussions. The reading will show you practical ways to make ethics a core part of your professional life, no matter your role. As you read, think about how these strategies could apply to your own career path.

💡 Tip: Skim the text first to capture the main message, then read closely for details that inspire you.

Reading Text – Building an Ethical Company

Adapted from Harvard Business Review



What does it mean to have an ethical company? Some people think ethics is just about following the rules, but it’s more than that. A company should not only avoid bad behaviour — it should also help people do the right thing. According to researchers Isaac H. Smith and Maryam Kouchaki, a workplace should be a place where employees can learn and grow as ethical professionals.

People are not born with fixed values. We learn values like respect, fairness or integrity by working with others and facing real situations. That’s why companies should talk about values every day, not only during training. Managers and leaders must help their teams understand what is right and what is wrong. For example, they ought to create a safe space where people feel comfortable to speak and reflect.

Work is a big part of life. You have to make good decisions not just when someone is watching, but all the time. You mustn’t think that ethics only matters in official documents. It matters in meetings, in emails, and in daily conversations.

Young workers or people with little experience may not know how to react in difficult situations. That’s normal. Companies should support them by giving examples, asking questions, and organising discussions about real problems. For example, they can talk about harassment, favouritism, or how to stop gossip at work. These are all behaviours that can damage a company’s culture.

Also, mentoring is a good way to share experience. A mentor ought to help younger workers think about their decisions and how to treat others with equality and accountability. If mentors don’t talk about values, the company loses an important opportunity to teach and support.

In short, if a company wants to build a positive and professional environment, it must include ethics in everyday life — not just in rules, but in actions.

💡 Tip: Read the text carefully to understand the key ideas about workplace ethics.

Reading Comprehension – Building an Ethical Company

Instructions: Read the text "Building an Ethical Company" and complete the activities below. Use the dropdowns or text inputs to answer, then click "Check Answers" for each activity to see feedback.

🧠 Exercise 1 – Multiple Choice: Main Ideas and Details

Instructions: Select the correct answer for each question based on the text. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 1. What does the text say an ethical company should do beyond following rules?
  • 2. According to the text, how do people learn values like respect and fairness?
  • 3. What should managers create to help employees discuss ethics?
  • 4. What is one way the text suggests supporting young workers?

💡 Tip: Focus on the main ideas and specific examples mentioned in the text to choose the correct answers.

🧠 Exercise 2 – True or False

Instructions: Decide if each statement is true or false based on the text. Select your answer from the dropdown. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 1. Ethics in a company only involves following official rules.
  • 2. Values like integrity are learned through workplace experiences.
  • 3. Managers should only discuss ethics during annual training sessions.
  • 4. Mentoring helps younger workers think about their decisions.

💡 Tip: Check the text for specific statements about ethics and workplace practices to determine if each statement is true or false.

🧠 Exercise 3 – Short Answer

Instructions: Answer each question in a complete sentence based on the text. Type your answer in the text box. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 1. Why should companies discuss values every day?
  • 2. What is one example of a behavior that can damage a company’s culture?
  • 3. How can mentors support younger workers in making ethical decisions?
  • 4. What is the main goal of building an ethical company, according to the text?

💡 Tip: Answer in complete sentences, summarizing or directly referencing the text. Minor wording variations are acceptable as long as the meaning is correct.

Use of English – Modal Verbs in Workplace Ethics

Welcome to Use of English!

In any workplace, communicating expectations, responsibilities, and ethical advice clearly and respectfully is essential. Modal verbs help you express what’s required, what’s forbidden, or what’s recommended when discussing professional values. In this section, you’ll explore how to use modal verbs to express:

  • Obligation – e.g., “You must report unethical behavior.”
  • Prohibition – e.g., “You mustn’t share confidential information.”
  • Advice and Recommendation – e.g., “We should promote fairness daily.”
  • Possibility and Permission – e.g., “Staff can suggest ethical improvements.”

We’ll draw examples from the reading text “Building an Ethical Company” and apply these verbs in professional scenarios, focusing on values like respect, equality, and accountability. This section is divided into three steps:

  • Diagnostic Test – Check your current understanding of modal verbs.
  • Grammar Lesson – Learn and reflect with real-world examples.
  • Grammar Practice – Consolidate your skills through guided activities.

💡 Tip: Think about how modal verbs shape ethical communication in your vocational field.

🎯 Why This Matters

Using modal verbs effectively will help you:

  • Communicate workplace rules and ethical advice with clarity and confidence.
  • Adapt to professional contexts, such as meetings or reports.
  • Express values like integrity and fairness in a precise, respectful way.

This section builds your Communicative Competence by enhancing your ability to express obligations and recommendations, Professional Competence by preparing you for ethical workplace communication, and Competence to Learn to Learn through self-assessment and targeted practice. Ready? Begin with the diagnostic test!

💡 Tip: Use the diagnostic test to identify areas for improvement in your grammar skills.

Introduction to Diagnostic Test

🎯 Get Ready to Check Your Grammar!

Before we dive into grammar explanations, let’s take a moment to see what you already know. This diagnostic test will check your understanding of three key grammar points used in professional communication about workplace ethics:

  • It is + adjective + to + verb – to give advice and recommendations, like suggesting ethical practices.
  • There is / There are – to present facts or possibilities, such as describing workplace policies.
  • One / We + verb – to speak generally or inclusively, often in formal or ethical discussions.

Imagine you’re advising a team on ethical behavior or describing your company’s values. These grammar structures help you communicate clearly and professionally. This test isn’t for marks—it’s a tool to boost your Competence to Learn to Learn and Competence for Lifelong Learning by identifying your strengths and areas to grow.

💡 Tip: Think of each question as a chance to practice communication in your vocational field.

🚀 What’s the Goal?

The test helps you decide if you need to review these grammar points or if you’re ready to move on to practice. Complete the activities, check your answers with the Answer Key, and calculate your score:

  • Score 80% or higher (16/20): You’re confident with these structures! Skip the grammar explanation and jump to the practice activities to apply your skills.
  • Score below 80%: Take a few minutes to review the grammar explanations—it’ll strengthen your professional communication skills.

This activity builds your Communicative Competence by practicing precise, ethical language and your Professional Competence by preparing you to express values in the workplace. Get started and take control of your learning!

💡 Tip: Read each item carefully, considering how the grammar reflects advice, facts, or general statements in an ethical context.

Diagnostic Test – Modal Verbs in Workplace Ethics

Instructions: Complete the test by choosing the correct modal verb, filling in the blanks, or correcting errors. There are 20 items. Click "Check Answers" for each part to see feedback. At the end, check your score. If you score 80% or higher (16/20), you can skip this grammar point.

Part A – Multiple Choice: Choose the Correct Modal

Instructions: Select the modal verb that best fits the sentence based on workplace ethics. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 1. Employees _____ report unethical behavior to maintain integrity.
  • 2. You _____ gossip about colleagues, as it harms team trust.
  • 3. New hires _____ complete mandatory ethics training.
  • 4. We _____ hold workshops to discuss workplace fairness.
  • 5. Ignoring minor issues _____ lead to a damaged company reputation.
  • 6. Employees _____ suggest improvements to the ethical guidelines.
  • 7. Mentors _____ guide younger workers to make fair decisions.

💡 Tip: Choose the modal that reflects the speaker’s stance—e.g., necessity (must, have to), prohibition (mustn’t), or possibility (could, might).

Part B – Sentence Completion

Instructions: Type the correct modal verb to complete each sentence about workplace ethics. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 8. Managers _____ create a safe space for ethical discussions.
  • 9. You _____ think ethics only matters in formal policies.
  • 10. Employees _____ attend optional diversity seminars, but they’re encouraged to.
  • 11. Companies _____ promote daily conversations about values.
  • 12. A small oversight _____ cause significant ethical issues later.
  • 13. Staff _____ report harassment anonymously if they feel uncomfortable.
  • 14. We _____ organize a meeting to address workplace conflicts.

💡 Tip: Use modals that match the ethical context—e.g., strong duty (must), recommendation (should), or possibility (might).

Part C – Find and Correct the Mistake

Instructions: Each sentence has one modal verb error. Type the corrected sentence in the text box. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 15. ❌ You can ignore complaints about favoritism in the workplace.
  • 16. ❌ Managers might create a safe space for ethical discussions.
  • 17. ❌ Employees don’t have to report unethical behavior to HR.
  • 18. ❌ We must discuss values only during annual training.
  • 19. ❌ Mentors can guide younger workers to make fair decisions.
  • 20. ❌ A small ethical lapse have to damage the company’s culture.

💡 Tip: Look for modal verbs that don’t match the ethical urgency or context, such as incorrect necessity or possibility.

Your Score

Click below to calculate your total score across all parts.

💡 Tip: A score of 80% or higher (16/20 correct) means you can skip this grammar point.

Grammar Focus: Modal Verbs in Workplace Ethics

What Are Modal Verbs?

Modal verbs let you express attitudes like necessity, possibility, permission, or advice—key for discussing ethics at work. In this session, we’ll explore how they shape professional communication, drawing from the reading “Building an Ethical Company” and real-world workplace scenarios.

Key modal verbs: must, mustn’t, have to, don’t have to, should, ought to, can, could, might.

💡 Tip: Focus on how these verbs reflect the speaker’s perspective on ethical choices.
🎧 Complementary Audio – Guided Explanation
This audio supports you as you go through the lesson. Listen to it for a step-by-step explanation with spoken examples. It’s especially helpful if you prefer learning by listening or want to review while doing other tasks.

🔍 Understanding Modal Verbs Through Meaning

Rather than memorizing rules, think about what these verbs reveal about the speaker’s view—how urgent, possible, or permissible an action is in the context of workplace ethics.

Modal Verb Meaning Example Interpretation
must Strong necessity, often driven by duty or ethics. Managers and leaders must help their teams understand what is right and what is wrong. The speaker sees this as a non-negotiable responsibility for ethical leadership.
have to External requirement, often from rules or norms. You have to make good decisions not just when someone is watching, but all the time. The speaker highlights a workplace expectation rooted in ethical standards.
mustn’t Strong prohibition, warning against harmful actions. You mustn’t think that ethics only matters in official documents. The speaker firmly rejects a limited view of ethics, emphasizing its daily importance.
don’t have to Lack of obligation, indicating freedom from requirement. Employees don’t have to attend optional ethics workshops, but they’re encouraged to. The speaker clarifies that participation is voluntary, offering flexibility.
should Recommendation, suggesting a beneficial action. Companies should talk about values every day. The speaker advises a practice that strengthens ethical culture, with some flexibility.
ought to Formal recommendation, often tied to moral duty. A mentor ought to help younger workers think about their decisions. The speaker gently urges mentors to guide ethically, with a sense of responsibility.
can Ability or permission, enabling ethical action. Employees can learn and grow as ethical professionals in a supportive workplace. The speaker highlights the opportunity for ethical development.
could Possibility or tentative suggestion for ethical choices. We could organize team discussions to address workplace gossip. The speaker proposes a potential strategy, leaving room for consideration.
might Uncertainty or low probability, often speculative. Ignoring harassment might damage the company’s reputation. The speaker warns of a possible negative outcome, urging caution.

💡 Tip: Notice how each modal shapes the speaker’s stance on ethics—some are firm, others flexible.

🗣️ Form and Use

Modal verbs are followed by a base verb and don’t change for person or number. Here’s how they work in professional contexts:

Affirmative

  • You must report unethical behavior to HR.
  • Employees have to complete ethics training annually.
  • We should promote fairness in team decisions.
  • Mentors ought to model respectful communication.
  • Staff can suggest improvements to the code of conduct.
  • We could hold a workshop on equality next month.
  • A small oversight might lead to bigger ethical issues.

Negative

  • You mustn’t ignore complaints about harassment.
  • Interns don’t have to lead ethics discussions.
  • You shouldn’t favor certain team members over others.
  • We can’t allow gossip to undermine team trust.

Questions

  • Should we revise our ethical guidelines?
  • Do managers have to monitor team conflicts?
  • Can employees report issues anonymously?
  • Could we invite a mentor to discuss integrity?
  • Might this decision affect our company’s reputation?

💡 Tip: Practice forming sentences to express your own ethical priorities.

Compare and Reflect

Look at these sentences—one from the text, one professional example:

  • From text: Managers and leaders must help their teams understand what is right and what is wrong.
  • Professional example: Employees can suggest improvements to the code of conduct.

Question: How do these modals differ?

Must conveys a strong, essential duty, signaling that ethical guidance is critical. Can opens up possibilities, inviting employees to contribute without pressure. Each reflects a different stance on ethical responsibility.

💡 Tip: Think about how word choice shapes expectations in your workplace.

🚀 Apply It

Reflect on these questions to connect modal verbs to your professional context:

  • What’s one thing you must do to maintain integrity in your vocational field?
  • Is there something you mustn’t do to avoid harming your workplace’s ethical culture?
  • What should a colleague do if they notice unfair treatment in your industry?
  • How can you contribute to a respectful work environment in your future role?
  • How might ignoring a small ethical issue affect your team in the long run?

💡 Tip: Use these questions to practice modals in sentences about your career goals.

Interactive Practice – Modal Verbs in Workplace Ethics

Instructions: Complete the activities below to practice using modal verbs (must, mustn’t, have to, don’t have to, should, ought to, can, could, might) in the context of workplace ethics. Use the dropdowns or text inputs to answer, then click "Check Answers" for each activity to see feedback.

🧠 Exercise 1 – Multiple Choice: Choose the Correct Modal

Instructions: Select the modal verb that best fits the sentence based on the context of workplace ethics. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 1. Leaders _____ create a safe space where employees feel comfortable discussing ethical issues.
  • 2. You _____ ignore reports of harassment, as it damages the company’s culture.
  • 3. Employees _____ attend optional ethics workshops, but they’re highly recommended.
  • 4. We _____ organize team discussions to address workplace favoritism.

💡 Tip: Choose the modal that matches the speaker’s stance—e.g., strong necessity (must), prohibition (mustn’t), or possibility (could).

🧠 Exercise 2 – Sentence Completion

Instructions: Type the correct modal verb to complete each sentence about workplace ethics. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 1. Companies _____ talk about values every day to foster an ethical culture.
  • 2. You _____ make decisions based on favoritism, as it undermines fairness.
  • 3. Mentors _____ help younger workers reflect on their ethical choices.
  • 4. Ignoring small ethical issues _____ lead to bigger problems in the company.

💡 Tip: Use modals that reflect the ethical urgency or possibility described—e.g., recommendation (should), prohibition (mustn’t), or uncertainty (might).

🧠 Exercise 3 – Apply Modals to Workplace Scenarios

Instructions: Read each workplace scenario and select the sentence with the correct modal verb to address the situation ethically. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 1. An employee notices gossip spreading in the team, which could harm morale.
  • 2. A manager sees an employee being treated unfairly by a colleague.
  • 3. A new intern is unsure how to handle a client’s unethical request.
  • 4. A team is considering whether to report a minor ethical lapse.

💡 Tip: Match the modal to the scenario’s ethical urgency—e.g., strong duty (must), suggestion (should), or possibility (could).

📚 Vocabulary – Workplace Values and Behaviours

Introduction

In every job, how we behave with others matters. Companies don’t just need people who know how to do their tasks — they also need professionals who act with respect, integrity, and fairness. In this section, you’ll learn key vocabulary to talk about positive values that help create a good work environment, and problematic behaviours that can damage trust and teamwork.

You will explore words that describe:

  • What companies should promote (like equality or accountability)
  • What professionals mustn’t do (like gossip or show favouritism)

This vocabulary will help you describe ethical situations, take part in discussions, and explain your point of view clearly and professionally. You’ll also connect the new words with your own experience in real or future workplaces.

💡 Tip: Think about how these words can help you communicate in your future job.

Vocabulary Focus – Workplace Values and Behaviours

Building an Ethical Workplace

Every workplace strives for fairness and respect, but challenges like unfair treatment or harmful behaviors can undermine these goals. Knowing how to describe positive values and identify problematic behaviors helps you communicate effectively, address issues professionally, and contribute to a better work environment.

In this session, you’ll learn vocabulary to:

  • Express core workplace values that promote fairness and trust.
  • Identify behaviors that harm teams or ethical standards.
  • Discuss ways to foster respect and accountability in professional settings.
  • Use precise terms to describe ethical expectations and challenges.

Let’s dive into two key areas: Core Workplace Values and Problematic Workplace Behaviours.

💡 Tip: Consider how these terms can help you communicate in your vocational field.

✅ Core Workplace Values

Word Meaning Example
respect Treating others with politeness and fairness Team members must show respect in all interactions.
integrity Acting honestly and ethically, even without supervision Leaders should demonstrate integrity in decision-making.
fairness Ensuring equal and unbiased treatment Performance reviews ought to be based on fairness.
equality Providing the same opportunities to everyone Companies should promote equality in hiring practices.
accountability Taking responsibility for one’s actions Employees have to show accountability for their mistakes.
trust Confidence in others’ reliability and honesty Building trust is essential for effective teamwork.
transparency Being open and honest in communication Managers can foster transparency by sharing clear goals.

💡 Tip: Use these words to describe positive actions in your workplace.

⚠️ Problematic Workplace Behaviours

Word Meaning Example
gossip Spreading rumors or talking about others behind their backs Gossip mustn’t be allowed to disrupt team morale.
harassment Unwanted, offensive behaviour toward someone Any form of harassment should be reported immediately.
favouritism Giving unfair advantages to certain individuals Managers mustn’t show favouritism in promotions.
bias Unfair prejudice or preference that distorts judgment Hiring decisions should be free from bias.
discrimination Treating people unfairly based on specific characteristics Discrimination can harm a company’s ethical culture.
negligence Failing to take proper care or responsibility Negligence in following ethical guidelines might lead to conflicts.

💡 Tip: These terms help you identify and discuss behaviours to avoid.

🚀 Apply Your Vocabulary

Reflect on these questions to connect the vocabulary to your professional context:

  • Which value (e.g., respect, integrity) is most important in your vocational field, and why?
  • Have you observed a problematic behaviour like gossip or bias in a workplace or school setting? How could it be addressed?
  • How can you use a term like transparency or accountability to describe an ethical action in your future role?

These reflections strengthen your Competence to Learn to Learn by connecting new vocabulary to real-world applications. Use these words in discussions, reports, or emails to communicate ethically and professionally.

💡 Tip: Practice using one value and one problematic behaviour in a sentence about your workplace.

Interactive Vocabulary Practice – Workplace Values and Behaviours

🧠 Activity 1 – Match and Complete

Part A – Match the Definitions

Instructions: Match each value or behavior to its definition by selecting from the dropdown. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 1. Fairness
  • 2. Gossip
  • 3. Accountability
  • 4. Harassment
  • 5. Transparency

Part B – Complete the Sentences

Instructions: Type the correct word from the box (respect, favoritism, integrity, discrimination, trust) to complete each sentence. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 6. In an ethical team, all members must show _____ in their work.
  • 7. The HR department must avoid any kind of _____ during hiring.
  • 8. A manager who gives extra benefits to a friend is showing _____.
  • 9. Employees need to act with _____ even when no one is watching.
  • 10. Team leaders have to build _____ by being consistent and honest.

💡 Tip: For Part A, match the term to its definition carefully. For Part B, choose a term from the box that fits the ethical context.

🧠 Activity 2 – Classify and Reflect

Instructions: Read each workplace situation and type the matching vocabulary term (from the values or behaviors list) in the text box. The type (Value or Behavior) is provided. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 1. A team leader shares decisions clearly with the group. (Value)
  • 2. An employee talks negatively about a coworker’s personal life. (Behavior)
  • 3. A company gives equal chances to all candidates. (Value)
  • 4. A manager ignores complaints from a junior employee. (Behavior)
  • 5. A supervisor gives the best shifts only to his friends. (Behavior)

💡 Tip: Use the type (Value or Behavior) to guide your choice of term—values are positive, behaviors are problematic.

🧠 Activity 3 – Listening: What’s the Problem? What’s the Value?

Instructions: Listen to each audio file. Each audio describes a workplace situation. Type the core value (applied or missing) and any problematic behavior mentioned or implied in the text boxes. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • Audio 1 – Receptionist
    Value missing:
    Behavior:
  • Audio 2 – Team Leader (Manufacturing)
    Value missing:
    Behavior:
  • Audio 3 – HR Intern
    Value missing:
    Behavior:
  • Audio 4 – Senior Technician (IT)
    Value applied:
    Behavior:
  • Audio 5 – Catering Assistant
    Value missing:
    Behavior:

💡 Tip: Listen carefully to identify the value being applied or missing and any problematic behavior described in the audio.

Estas imágenes han sido creadas exclusivamente para uso educativo y no reproducen contenido preexistente. No están sujetas a derechos de autor de terceros y pueden ser utilizadas dentro del marco del proyecto educativo sin fines comerciales. Se aplican los términos de uso y licencias correspondientes al servicio de generación de imágenes de OpenAI, que permiten su uso para fines educativos y de desarrollo de materiales propios.
Unit 5: Professional values and ethics - Session 1
